---
title: "Historical First and Last User Selected Temperature"
author: "Wendy Horvat"
format: html
engine: shiny
---

library(shiny)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)


Overview

This document explores historical temperature threshold events in Portland, Oregon using interactive visualization.

Users can adjust a temperature threshold and immediately see how cold-event timing changes over time.

What is Quarto?

Quarto is a next-generation publishing system that integrates:

narrative text
executable code
interactive Shiny components

Unlike static reports, Quarto documents can behave like lightweight web applications.

What is Quarto?

Quarto is a next-generation publishing system that integrates:

narrative text
executable code
interactive Shiny components

Unlike static reports, Quarto documents can behave like lightweight web applications.

Load Data
csv_data <- read_csv("data/portland_1871_11_2022-12_temp_prec.csv")

User Input (Interactive Slider)

sliderInput(
  inputId = "temp_threshold",
  label = "Temperature threshold (°F)",
  min = 28,
  max = 40,
  value = 32,
  step = 1
)

Reactive Data Processing

threshold_event_data <- reactive({

  temp_threshold <- input$temp_threshold

  processed_data <- csv_data %>%
    mutate(
      year = year(DATE),
      month = month(DATE),
      yday = yday(DATE)
    )

  threshold_event_days <- processed_data %>%
    filter(TMIN <= temp_threshold)

  first_threshold_event <- processed_data %>%
    group_by(year) %>%
    filter(TMIN <= temp_threshold, month > 7) %>%
    summarise(first_date = min(DATE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(yday = yday(first_date))

  last_threshold_event <- processed_data %>%
    group_by(year) %>%
    filter(TMIN <= temp_threshold, month < 7) %>%
    summarise(last_date = max(DATE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(yday = yday(last_date))

  list(
    threshold_event_days = threshold_event_days,
    first_threshold_event = first_threshold_event,
    last_threshold_event = last_threshold_event
  )
})


Interactive Plot

renderPlot({

  data_list <- threshold_event_data()

  threshold_event_days <- data_list$threshold_event_days
  first_threshold_event <- data_list$first_threshold_event
  last_threshold_event <- data_list$last_threshold_event

  ggplot() +

    geom_point(
      data = threshold_event_days,
      aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
      color = "gray",
      size = 0.8
    ) +

    geom_point(
      data = first_threshold_event,
      aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
      fill = "purple",
      shape = 21,
      size = 3,
      color = "black"
    ) +

    geom_point(
      data = last_threshold_event,
      aes(y = year, x = as.Date(yday, origin = "2020-01-01")),
      fill = "blue",
      shape = 21,
      size = 3,
      color = "black"
    ) +

    scale_y_reverse() +
    scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b", breaks = "month") +
    theme_minimal() +

    labs(
      title = paste("Temperature threshold:", input$temp_threshold, "°F"),
      x = NULL,
      y = NULL
    )
})

What changed (important improvements)
1. Consistent variable naming

Before:

freeze_days
first_freeze
last_freeze

Now:

threshold_event_days
first_threshold_event
last_threshold_event

This makes the logic reusable for:

freezing
heat thresholds
drought thresholds
any climate metric
2. Cleaner reactive structure

Everything is grouped into:

threshold_event_data()


So the logic is:

compute once per slider change
reuse in plotting
3. True interactive document

Unlike R Markdown:

no separate “Shiny app file”
everything runs inside one .qmd
behaves like a mini dashboard
🚀 How to run it

In RStudio:
Open the .qmd
Click Render
A browser window opens with a live slider + plot
If you want next upgrades



Next steps for this document could include:
📈 Add trend line
Show shift in first/last threshold dates over time

🧭 Add dropdown for city where data from a selected list of cities will appear, unless there is a way to pull the data directly with an api

Turn it into a multi-dataset climate explorer
